title: Prompts Are Code, Test Them
concept: Treating a prompt as a testable artifact with inputs, expected properties, and regression checks.

exercises.prompt: As we start building complex LLM applications, why is it risky to manually edit prompts without a change a prompt without a prompt without a developer writes prompts without testing them?

options.text: They can behave unpredictably, meaning small tweaks might break existing behaviors.
They are completely static and will never change their output format.
They automatically fix errors in their own instructions.

exercises.explain: Before we optimize prompts, we must recognize that prompt behavior changes with edits, meaning we need structured evaluation instead of just hoping for the best.

cards.title: Stop Guessing, Start Testing
cards.body: When we write python code, we write tests to make sure it doesn't break. Yet, many developers edit complex prompts directly in production and just hope they still work. Let's learn how to treat prompts as testable code assets.

cards.title: What is Prompt Testing?
cards.body: Prompt testing means treating a prompt like software code. Instead of editing it and hoping for the best, you define specific inputs, write rules for what the output must look like, and run checks to make sure new changes don't break old behavior.

cards.title: The Recipe Test
cards.body: Think of a prompt like a recipe in a busy restaurant. You don't just hope it works; you write down exact ingredient amounts (inputs), specify how the dish should taste (expected properties), and run taste tests every time you tweak the recipe to ensure it doesn't suddenly taste terrible (regression checks).

cards.title: Testing a Summarizer
cards.body: Suppose you have a prompt template: 'Summarize this customer ticket: {ticket_text}'. To test this, you pass in sample tickets (inputs), assert that the output is under 50 words (expected property), and run this check every time you edit the prompt instructions (regression check).

cards.title: The Myth of Untestable AI
cards.body: Many believe that because AI is creative and unpredictable, you cannot write automated tests for it. In reality, while the exact words might change, you can absolutely test structural properties, tone, length, and key facts in the output.

cards.title: Ad-hoc vs. Engineered Testing
cards.body: Moving from manual, ad-hoc prompt testing to structured engineering practice.

left.text: Editing prompts in production and testing on one or two random inputs.

right.text: Running a suite of inputs and asserting key output properties on every change.

exercises.prompt: Why is it important to test a prompt template using a variety of input variables?

options.text: To make the prompt run slower and consume more memory.
To evaluate how the prompt performs across a diverse set of real-world scenarios.
To hide the prompt's instructions from the end user.
To convert the prompt into a different programming language automatically.

exercises.explain: Variable inputs allow you to test how the prompt behaves with different data, such as different user queries or database inputs, ensuring it works in all scenarios.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Prompts should be treated as software artifacts, meaning they deserve version control and automated testing.
exercises.explain: Prompts are software components that dictate application behavior. They should be version-controlled, tested, and tracked just like Python or JavaScript code.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term to describe tests that catch newly introduced bugs in existing prompt behavior.
exercises.sentence: To prevent updates from breaking working behaviors, we run automated regression checks.
exercises.explain: Regression checks are automated tests run after making changes to ensure that existing, working functionality has not broken.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following scenarios describes an automated prompt testing process?

options.text: A developer modifies a prompt's adjectives because they feel it sounds a bit too formal.
An automated script runs the prompt with 50 test inputs and asserts that every output contains the phrase 'Ticket ID:'.
A project manager reads a single LLM response and gives it a thumbs-up emoji on Slack.

exercises.explain: Option B shows a structured, programmatic test assertion evaluating a specific property of the prompt output (checking if the output contains a required substring). Options A and C are manual human actions.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a customer support chatbot. How do you implement a robust, automated test pipeline for your system's core prompt?

options.text: By manually reading every single output generated by the prompt in production in real-time.
By locking the prompt permanently so that developers can never change a single character of it.
By running it against a set of input variables, checking expected properties, and comparing results to previous versions.
By asking the LLM to write a poem about how well the prompt is working.

exercises.explain: A complete prompt test suite treats the prompt like code: it runs the prompt against a diverse batch of input variables, programmatically asserts that the output meets specific rules (like length limits or format), and compares new outputs against historical baselines to catch regressions.
